chapters.title: The physical world we overlook
chapters.text:
Have you ever wondered what your phone, home, and city are built from? Glance around and you will spot dozens of materials composing daily reality. What would life look like if they vanished overnight?

Modern culture obsesses over financial freedom and spiritual growth, yet this planet is fundamentally material. It is made of stuff — without which we would lack shelter, tools, and containers for food. Remove metal, paper, and glass and you erase entertainment, education, and much of modern work. Without textiles we might still wear fur or freeze, because a leaf is not a winter coat.

For centuries, materials extended survival and elevated living standards. Human eras took names from discoveries — Bronze Age, Iron Age. Ancestors treated matter with reverence. Today clay huts became skyscrapers, missing teeth became ceramic replacements, and sticks for play became touchscreens.

Recycle old metal items to reduce waste and save resources.

The chapters ahead reveal how the substances surrounding us shape civilization — and how deeply our lives depend on them.

chapters.title: Steel on trial
chapters.text:
Humans worked metal for millennia yet grasped its science only in the twentieth century. Metals consist of opaque crystal structures. Atomic defects — dislocations — let metal bend instead of snap. Billions of dislocations inside a paper clip allow flexibility; a razor dulls when hair disrupts ordered crystals.

Metalworking began when ancestors heated green malachite rock into copper. That breakthrough powered civilizations — Ancient Egyptians carved pyramid stones with copper chisels.

Gold arrived too. Soft and easily scratched, jewelry alloys blend gold with silver or copper; pure gold rarely sits in your ring. Scarcity keeps gold precious.

Steel — iron plus carbon — revolutionized tools through affordability and strength. Early makers guessed ratios by trial and error. Societies mastering steel prospered. In Japan, forging a samurai sword became ritual. Blades combined:

• Low-carbon steel at the core — tough, resistant to breaking.
• High-carbon steel at the edge — brittle yet razor sharp.

Japan led until the Industrial Revolution demanded ships, bridges, and rails. Henry Bessemer's process enabled mass steel production, penetrating machinery and households alike. In 1903, King Camp Gillette sold disposable razors, reshaping grooming and building an empire.

Clean and store metal items properly to extend their lifespan.

Progress yielded stainless steel needing no paint. Harry Brearley found that precise carbon and chromium ratios produced self-healing rust resistance — even after scratching. Cutlery, sinks, and appliances followed.

From skyscrapers to scalpels, steel's story is one of experimentation rewarded — a reminder that the objects we treat as mundane rest on centuries of curious failure and refinement.

chapters.title: A clean sheet for paper
chapters.text:
We cherish books, newspapers, and posters yet rarely consider their manufacture. Most paper begins with trees containing cellulose fibers and lignin glue. Production steps include:

1. Chopping wood into chips
2. Boiling and chemically separating fibers from stubborn lignin — like removing gum from hair
3. Creating watery pulp with cellulose suspended like noodles
4. Drying fibers
5. Producing brown stock, then brightening it for offices, homes, and bookstores

Paper's future sparks fierce debate among readers. Kindle advocates claim e-ink mimics pages without felling trees — a sacrifice-free era. Others insist nothing replaces paper's smell and texture. A Le Trefle toilet-paper ad called "Emma" on YouTube even jokes about how intimate paper can feel.

Life without paper would erase beloved objects:

• Photos: tangible memory anchors; without photo paper, early childhood might fade from view.
• Wrapping paper: tearing gifts under a Christmas tree forms cherished childhood joy.
• Banknotes: cotton-based currency survives washing — among the world's most desired crafted objects.
• Love letters: you touch what a beloved touched; if romance ends, you can burn the memento.

Choose products with eco-friendly packaging to reduce waste.

Did you know? Instead of cutting trees, the Ukrainian Releaf Paper company processes fallen leaves gathered in urban areas into sustainable paper products like bags and cardboard, reducing CO2 emissions by 78%. Its clients include L'oreal, Schneider Electric, Samsung, and NYX.

chapters.title: No plastic, no equality
chapters.text:
What links billiard balls to modern cinema? Plastic. In the late nineteenth century, combs and toothbrushes cost a fortune because ivory dominated luxury goods. John Wesley Hyatt answered a ten-thousand-dollar prize seeking an ivory substitute for billiard balls and created nitrocellulose — a moldable, affordable material.

Alexander Parkes invented Parkesine first, but Hyatt — backed by General Lefferts, who also invested in young Thomas Edison — patented celluloid and built a thriving company. Later lawsuits from Daniel Spill over Xylonite ended with judges denying exclusive patents, inviting competition.

By the 1870s celluloid spread globally. Ebony and ivory yielded to early plastics; exclusivity gave way to mass production and broader access.

Nitrocellulose then transformed photography. Heavy glass plates limited family portraits until George Eastman developed celluloid film and compact Kodak cameras — memories on a plastic scroll for ordinary households.

. . . materials are a reflection of who we are, a multiscale expression of our human needs and desires. ~ Mark Miodownik, PhD

Flexible transparent film enabled motion pictures: rapid sequences projected moving images. The Lumière brothers launched cinema by realizing audiences could watch stories unfold on screen.

Purchase biodegradable plastic products to reduce environmental impact.

Later plastics multiplied:

• Bakelite powered radios, TVs, and telephones.
• Nylon replaced silk stockings and spawned new fabrics.
• Vinyl delivered music with distinctive warmth — some insist vinyl is the only "real" listening experience.
• Silicone gained fame through 1990s celebrity culture and advanced cosmetic surgery.

chapters.title: Discovering new worlds through glass
chapters.text:
Books and travel transport you culturally; a telescope opens entire planets. Microscopes revealed structures inside every organism and object, enabling genetic engineering and assisted reproduction — all impossible without glass.

Glass forms from silicon dioxide. Quartz crystals in sand arrange like water molecules, yet behave differently under heat.

Heating activates molecules until bonds break at the melting point. Water refreezes into ordered ice; quartz molecules scatter like children after recess and cool into chaotic glass rather than crystal.

Rome advanced glassmaking most — windows, affordable mirrors, and blowing techniques for delicate wine glasses.

Eastern civilizations mastered steel, paper, and ceramics yet neglected glass, giving the West an edge in the scientific revolution. Telescopes, test tubes, and labware all depend on it.

Today glass anchors housing, transport, and research. In the 1960s automakers sought safer windshields; shattered glass injured passengers. Rapid cooling of molten glass produced toughened panes.

Glass became the foundation for all scientific discoveries.

Laminated glass added plastic layers so shards stayed bonded — leading to bullet-resistant windows that can blunt even AK-47 fire through a cake-like sandwich of materials.

chapters.title: What is a lover's drink?
chapters.text:
In a 2007 study, Dr. David Lewis compared couples kissing versus eating chocolate, monitoring heart and brain activity. Kissing raised heart rate more, yet chocolate produced stronger, longer-lasting brain responses in both regions.

Most people love chocolate without knowing its unromantic journey from cocoa pod to candy bar. Pods grow on tree trunks; each holds thirty to forty almond-shaped seeds. Harvesters cut them with machetes and leave them to ferment on the ground.

Heat drives decomposition that shapes flavor — without fermentation, chocolate cannot form. Weather, ripeness, and pile height make the process finicky.

The material world is not just a display of our technology and culture, it is part of us. ~ Mark Miodownik, PhD

Mesoamericans brewed chocolatl — roasted fermented cocoa with hot water — as a ceremonial aphrodisiac.

European explorers imported it, but bitterness and oil (half of each bean is fat) repelled palates until 1828, when Dutch Van Houten separated cocoa powder from fat, creating velvety drinks.

Powder-and-fat separation enabled solid bars. Fry and Sons combined elements into melt-in-mouth chocolate; antioxidants aided shelf life while sugar sweetened the experience. Swiss Nestlé milk powder later commercialized milk chocolate globally.

Substitute sugary desserts with a piece of dark chocolate.

Modern chocolate varies wildly in additives and fillings. Despite nutritional drawbacks, consumption remains especially high across Northern Europe.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
However lofty our ideas become, nothing functions without the tangible world around us. Without paper, literacy culture stalls. Without toughened glass, offices grow dim and fragile. Without chocolate… life loses a familiar pleasure.

Every tool you touch encodes generations of experimentation and craft. Respecting materials is part of respecting human ingenuity. Before you resent an old car or dismiss newspaper readers, pause to acknowledge the comfort, freedom, and knowledge those objects made possible.

Try this

• Cultivate mindfulness when buying and throwing things away.
• Think about materials that make your life richer and in what way. Learn to appreciate it.
• Watch The Future of Materials lecture by Mark Miodownik on Youtube.
